summary: Gruber examines Apple's latest round of price increases for its media services, including a near-doubling of Apple TV+ since launch. He frames the broader streaming price hike trend through the lens of piracy as collective bargaining, arguing that user experience — not just price — determines whether people pay or pirate. He defends TV+ and Arcade price increases as justified given their quality, but criticizes News+ for delivering a terrible ad-laden reading experience unworthy of a premium subscription. He closes by noting the irony that Apple steadily raises media prices while iCloud storage tiers haven't changed since 2015.
key_points: Media piracy functions as ad hoc collective bargaining between consumers and corporations — pricing and user experience both factor into whether people pay or bootleg
Apple TV+ has become the new HBO with consistently high-quality originals and multi-season commitments, justifying its price increase
Apple News+ delivers a miserable ad-laden reading experience that makes free web browsing with ad blockers superior, making its price hike indefensible
Netflix's cultural commitment to great user experience — fast apps, reliable sign-ins, good design — is vastly under-remarked-upon as a competitive advantage against piracy
Apple steadily raises media subscription prices annually while iCloud storage tiers have remained unchanged since 2015, which feels stubborn and spiteful
money_quotes: I've always thought of media bootlegging as a form of negotiation. It's like ad hoc collective bargaining between users and the corporations that set the prices and define the experience of consuming the media.

Every time the official service makes you watch an unskippable ad, every time their shitty custom video player makes fast-forwarding or rewinding janky, every time their shitty app feels slow or forgets where you were in a movie that you paused days ago — those little annoyances add up, and they reduce the user experience advantage that paying for content should provide compared to bootlegging it.

99 cents was an appealing price, but not as appealing as free. But the overall experience of searching for and downloading music from iTunes was so much better than bootlegging that it made you happy to pay for the songs.

You can tell that everyone at Netflix, right up to Ted Sarandos and Reed Hastings, cares about the entire user experience. Netflix, culturally, is much like Apple in that regard: they have good taste baked into the company DNA.

I've been saying for a few years that Apple TV+ is becoming the new HBO — a channel dedicated to prestigious original content, quality over quantity — and that feels more true than ever now that HBO is being watered down inside Max.
Some streaming apps are so bad they make it feel like as much work to watch their content legitimately as it would be to pirate it.
As with bootlegging media and pirating games and apps, ad blocking is a negotiation between users and publishers.
Steady increases in media subscription pricing are annoying but understandable. Never increasing the amount of storage most users get with iCloud feels stubborn and spiteful.
key_insight: The battle against piracy is won not primarily through pricing but through user experience — and Apple undermines its own case with News+ by delivering an ad-riddled reading experience worse than free alternatives.
